content.problemStatement:
Consider the following function fff, defined on pairs of boolean values:

f(x,y)={1, if x=y=00, otherwisef(x, y) = \begin{cases}
1, \text{ if } x = y = 0 \\
0, \text{ otherwise}
\end{cases}f(x,y)={1, if x=y=00, otherwise‚ÄãSo f(0,0)=1f(0, 0) = 1f(0,0)=1, and for any other input f(x,y)=0f(x, y) = 0f(x,y)=0.

You're given a binary string SSS of length NNN. You can perform the following operation on it:


Choose an index iii (1<i<N1 \lt i \lt N1<i<N) and do **both**:

Set Si‚àí1=f(Si‚àí1,Si)S_{i-1} = f(S_{i-1}, S_i)Si‚àí1‚Äã=f(Si‚àí1‚Äã,Si‚Äã)
Set Si+1=f(Si+1,Si)S_{i+1} = f(S_{i+1}, S_i)Si+1‚Äã=f(Si+1‚Äã,Si‚Äã)


Find the **maximum** possible number of ones that SSS can have after performing several such operations (possibly, none).

Also, find a sequence of at most 3N3N3N operations that attains this maximum.

It can be proved that such a sequence always exists.

Note that you do not have to minimize the number of operations.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT ‚Äî the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚Äî the length of the string.
The second line of each test case contains the binary string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, output two lines:



On the first line, print a single integer KKK (0‚â§K‚â§3N0 \leq K \leq 3N0‚â§K‚â§3N): the number of moves you are going to make.
On the second line, print KKK space-separated integers i1,i2,‚Ä¶,iKi_1, i_2, \ldots, i_Ki1‚Äã,i2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,iK‚Äã, denoting the indices chosen by you to perform the operation, in sequence.

Each of these indices should be >1\gt 1>1 and <N\lt N<N.

Note that if K=0K = 0K=0, you may either leave the second line blank, or choose to skip it entirely.


If there are multiple valid outputs, you may print any of them.
constraints: SSS is a binary string, i.e, contains only the characters `0` and `1`.
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 3‚ãÖ1053\cdot 10^53‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We perform a single operation choosing i=4i = 4i=4. This does the following:

Set S3=f(S3,S4)=f(0,0)=1S_3 = f(S_3, S_4) = f(0, 0) = 1S3‚Äã=f(S3‚Äã,S4‚Äã)=f(0,0)=1.
Set S5=f(S5,S4)=f(0,0)=1S_5 = f(S_5, S_4) = f(0, 0) = 1S5‚Äã=f(S5‚Äã,S4‚Äã)=f(0,0)=1.

The string is now 111011111011111011, and contains 555 ones.
This is the best we can do - it can be shown that attaining 666 ones is impossible.
Test case 222: We perform two operations:

Choose i=2i = 2i=2, which updates S1S_1S1‚Äã and S3S_3S3‚Äã. The string is now 100011110001111000111.
Choose i=3i = 3i=3, which updates S2S_2S2‚Äã and S4S_4S4‚Äã. The string is now 110111111011111101111.

This is the best we can do.
Test case 333: Every character is already 111, so nothing needs to be done.
Test case 444: Choosing i=2i = 2i=2 (our only possible move) results in the string 001001001.
This has one 111, and is the best we can do.
Note that the original string also had 111 one, but since we aren't aiming to minimize the number of operations, this output is accepted too.
